As for the balance sheet, this company has about $4.14 billion in debt and around $2.94 billion in cash. I view this as a strong balance sheet with lots of cash, and most importantly is the possibility that Snap could be profitable going forward for the first time. That could lead to a situation where the company could pay down debt and even buy back shares as Irenic Capital is suggesting.

Other Points To Consider

Here are a few other points that should be considered when it comes to Snap:

  1. I am pleased to see Irenic Capital buying shares of Snap, and also seeing ideas about cost cutting which seem to align with my own views. I also viewed major investments in this company by Prince Alwaleed bin Talal as a sign of major potential upside, as he is said to own or control over 40 million shares which is nearly 3% of the company. However, I am troubled that I have not seen any insider buying in recent weeks, and multiple insiders have recently sold Snap shares even in the $4 range.
  2. The European Union announced an investigation into Snap to see if it was complying with the Digital Services Act. The investigation is said to be focused on Snap's age verification system and other child protection concerns. Based on the fact that Snap requires its users to be at least 13 years old and that it has additional privacy protections for anyone between age 13-17, I feel that the company has taken appropriate measures to protect teens under the Digital Services Act.
  3. Snap has a dual-class structure that gives founders like CEO Evan Spiegel total control. This limits the power of outside investors including shareholder activists like Irenic Capital.

Where I Could Be Wrong

I am concerned that Snap management will continue on the same path which has led to losses for many shareholders. If the company continues funding and losing money on potential "moonshots" like Specs, it could diminish profitability for years to come. This company is much smaller than Meta Platforms and YouTube and it does not have the same level of financial resources to fund R&D or to defend lawsuits if these addiction type legal claims keep coming and if they are not overturned on appeal.

Snap typically appeals to a younger audience which is ideal for some advertisers, but younger consumers also don't typically have as much spending power. This could limit Snap's appeal for advertisers. The U.S. economy appears to be slowing down and the job market has been weakening in recent months. This could lead to a recession, and that could result in ad spending cutbacks for Snap. Also, user growth has recently been slower in North America and if that trend continues, it could become a bigger concern for shareholders and advertisers.

In Summary

I view Snap as a higher risk but potentially very high reward stock. At this point, there is just around $4 worth of remaining downside, if the stock goes to zero. By contrast, I believe that a return to $26 per share is realistic as outlined by Irenic Capital, but only if management takes some major steps to reduce headcount and cut back on other expenses like Specs.

Even before Irenic Capital sent out its open letter, I was already thinking (and I believe other investors were as well), that Snap might announce major AI-related layoffs when it announces Q1 2026 earnings on April 27th, 2026. Block shares soared after that company announced major layoffs, and we know that Meta Platforms shares have seen significant gains when it announced a "year of efficiency" in 2022. I think it might be time for Evan Spiegel to do the same with Snap, and I believe this could be a major upside catalyst for this very depressed stock.

data.title: SOLZ: Structurally Inefficient Given The Emergence Of Spot Solana ETFs
data.description:
It has been a little over one year since the first futures-based exchange-traded funds tied to the price of Solana came to market, with the Volatility Shares Solana ETF (SOLZ) beginning to trade on March 20th, 2025. Unlike spot ETFs [which at the time of the fund’s release had not yet received regulatory approval], the Volatility Shares ETF tracks the price of Solana through futures contracts rather than holding SOL-USD directly.

Similar to other cryptocurrency spot-based exchange-traded funds, the release of futures-based ETFs served as a precursor for the eventual approval of spot Solana ETFs near the end of 2025. With that said, given the emergence of spot ETFs, the use case for funds like SOLZ has become limited. I argue that, due to the fund’s higher expense ratio, lack of access to staking yield, and structural inefficiencies, investors should largely steer clear of SOLZ. While I are generally optimistic about the prospects of the underlying cryptocurrency, I assign a “Sell” rating to the Volatility Shares Solana ETF.

In the current bear market, I acknowledge that, despite long-term fundamentals remaining intact for the underlying token, near-term price action may continue to face headwinds. Given the current risk-off market environment, I maintain hold ratings on both spot Solana ETFs and the underlying asset.

Unique Value Proposition of Solana Futures-Based ETF has Eroded With Emergence of Spot ETFs

SOLZ does not directly hold Solana tokens. Instead, according to the issuer, “[the fund uses] financial derivatives [primarily futures contracts and potentially swap agreements] to gain exposure to Solana's price movements.”

According to the fund’s prospectus, “SOLZ seeks long-term capital appreciation by actively managing positions in Solana futures traded on CFTC-regulated exchanges.” As a result, investors' returns are tied to the performance of the futures contracts rather than the underlying Solana itself.

Given the greater efficiency of spot ETFs, it is my opinion that the qualities of SOLZ have become structurally disadvantaged in the current environment. Volatility Shares acknowledges that the fund aims to provide investors 1X exposure to the price movements of Solana while avoiding many of the difficulties/complexities of direct crypto ownership. However, the question becomes, in the current market, why would an investor choose a futures-based fund rather than a spot-based fund?

SOLZ’s structural problems stem from its reliance on derivatives rather than holding spot Solana, which has resulted in deviations in performance over time. In particular, losses can occur from selling lower-priced expiring contracts and buying higher-priced, longer-dated ones. Over time, this can create a persistent drag on returns.

Additionally, a portion of the portfolio must remain in cash and treasuries as collateral, which limits the full participation in Solana’s upside price appreciation. When combined with higher fees and price tracking inefficiencies, these structural factors can lead to a return profile that fails to match that of the spot-based ETFs. These inefficiencies are structural rather than cyclical and therefore unlikely to improve over time.

The chart below shows that during periods of higher market volatility, SOLZ’s performance has deviated from that of both GSOL and SOL-USD. For example, performance between SOLZ and GSOL differed by approximately 34 percentage points in September 2025.

Another structural disadvantage comes in the form of SOLZ’s dividend. With a current dividend yield of 3.30%, this is derived from the cash and treasuries held by the fund rather than the underlying economics of the Solana network itself. In contrast, spot Solana ETFs that offer staking capabilities can generate yield directly from on-chain activity. Notably, given current staking yields (~6.2%), the potential income derived from staking is higher than the dividend provided by SOLZ. This makes SOLZ’s income stream both less competitive and structurally disconnected from the underlying asset.

While staking fees vary significantly across funds (see table below), current net yields for spot ETFs are still significantly greater than SOLZ’s current dividend yield. As an example, the Franklin Solana ETF (SOEZ) currently offers a net yield of 5.42%.

ETF

Staking Fees

BSOL

6% of Solana rewards

FSOL

15% of Solana rewards

VSOL

0.28% of total staked Solana

GSOL

23% of Solana rewards

(Source: etfdb)

One major drawback to the Volatility Shares Solana ETF is the expense ratio of 0.95%. When compared to spot ETFs, only the REX-Osprey SOL + Staking ETF (SSK) has a worse fee structure. As shown in the chart below, the Franklin Solana ETF (SOEZ) and the Bitwise Solana Staking ETF (BSOL) are currently the most affordable options for investors.

Additionally, as I have discussed in previous crypto ETF articles, AUM remains critically important given the burgeoning and highly competitive new asset class. By a wide margin, BSOL remains the spot Solana ETF leader, with just under $600 million in assets. That said, SOLZ maintains a respectable AUM of $101.20 million.

However, as crypto continues to navigate the current risk-off environment, I expect capital to likely migrate from futures-based ETFs like SOLZ to spot funds like BSOL, FSOL, and SOEZ as they prove to be more efficient vehicles to invest in the underlying crypto.

Given the introduction of spot Solana ETFs, the original purpose of SOLZ has largely been eliminated. Now, investors can invest directly from their brokerage accounts in funds that own the underlying asset. As a result, SOLZ now represents a structurally inferior and increasingly unnecessary option for investors seeking Solana exposure.

Risks to Underlying Thesis

Despite the structural drawbacks I have covered in this article, SOLZ may retain relevance for certain market participants.

For investors who are comfortable with exposure via derivatives, its accessibility via traditional brokerage accounts continues to provide a frictionless way to gain exposure to Solana’s price movements. SOLZ maintains respectable liquidity and AUM, supporting efficient trading and minimizing execution risk. Short-term traders or individuals seeking exposure without the complexities of spot ownership or staking may choose the futures-based fund over other available options.

Lastly, the fund’s collateral-driven yield derived from cash and treasuries could become relatively more attractive if staking yields were to decline and interest rates were to rise. This could potentially narrow the advantage currently held by spot-based staking ETFs. While I view SOLZ as structurally disadvantaged for long-term investors, it may still be appropriate in more tactical/constrained use cases.

Solana Network

The Solana network continues to be one of the leading L1s, with strong underlying on-chain metrics. Stablecoin supply remains near all-time highs of $15 billion, representative of a strong liquidity base. Likewise, TVL remains significantly elevated when compared to 2023 lows, currently sitting at approximately $6.4 billion.

Despite the current risk-off environment, Solana has retained a strong base of users and network activity, representative of its real economic value. With a demonstrated history of throughput advantages and clear use cases across DeFi, stablecoins, and RWAs, it is my opinion that it is likely to maintain its position among the leading cryptocurrencies. However, as I have seen in the past, price performance and network strength do not always move in tandem. In the short term, risk remains elevated.

While long-term fundamentals remain intact for the underlying token, near-term price action may continue to face headwinds. In the current market environment, the investment case for Solana remains mixed. While the network continues to show strong underlying activity, fundamentals such as active number of addresses and TVL have yet to translate into meaningful upward price movement amid the current risk-off backdrop. Given that crypto tends to underperform during periods of tightening financial conditions and reduced liquidity, I continue to remain cautious on the underlying crypto and spot ETFs.

Final Takeaway

While the Solana network continues to demonstrate strong fundamentals and long-term potential, SOLZ is no longer the best way for long-term investors to capture that upside. The fund’s reliance on futures introduces structural complications such as tracking inefficiencies and limited yield that is disconnected from the underlying network. When combined with a higher expense ratio, I view SOLZ’s return profile as being inherently less efficient relative to spot-based alternatives.

As the market continues to mature and capital flows toward more efficient structures, spot ETFs seem increasingly better suited for long-term investors. While I am generally optimistic about the long-term prospects of the underlying cryptocurrency, I assign a “Sell” rating to SOLZ.

data.title: Modine: Breakout Frenzy Meets AI Beneficiary Status - Downgrade To Hold
data.description:
I previously covered Modine Manufacturing Company (MOD) in January 2026, discussing why I had reiterated my Buy rating, thanks to the likely to be durable cooling demand across its diversified end markets and the top/bottom-line accretion from the expanded manufacturing capacity/improved pricing power.

This was despite the near-term risks from the potential sideways trading through FQ3'26, attributed to the pulled-forward upside potential and the elevated short interest ratio.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am downgrading the MOD stock to a Hold here, with the January 2026 breakout occurring overly fast/furiously while triggering the February 2026 bull trap and the still expensive stock valuations.

Pending a further correction to my Buy Zones, I am of the opinion that there remains a mixed risk/reward profile at current inflated levels, despite their profitable growth prospects during the multi-year data center capex boom along with the tailwinds from the upcoming Performance Technologies spin-off.

MOD Proves Its AI Beneficiary Status

Since my last Buy rating, MOD has had an outsized stock price performance while hitting a new 52-week high of $243s before returning some of those gains to retest the $200s by the time of writing.

If anything, readers may want to note that a similar breakout and moderation have also been observed in its direct thermal management peers in varying degrees.

1. Multi-Year Data Center Super Cycle

Much of their tailwinds may be attributed to the Q4'25 earnings season reiterating the durability of the AI spending trends, as the four leading hyperscalers "now expecting combined spending of close to $700 billion" on data center-related capex entering 2026.

These elevated spending trends are likely to be a multi-year cadence as well, as market analysts already project the global data center capacity to "nearly double from 103 GW to 200 GW by 2030," with it naturally triggering tailwinds for numerous data center-related infrastructure stocks, MOD included.

The same has been reported by MOD in the recent FQ3'26 earnings call, as the management raised their FY2026 net sales guidance to +22.5% YoY at the midpoint and adj. EBITDA to +18.5% YoY, against the original guidance of +6% YoY and +11% YoY offered in the FQ4'25 earnings call, respectively,

It is apparent that the management's intensified focus into the data center opportunities has paid off handsomely, as observed in the data center-related revenue growth by +78% YoY and the Climate Solutions' revenue growth by +50.9% YoY in FQ3'26.

This is significantly aided by the ongoing ramp-up of their capacity expansion, allowing them to capitalize on the ongoing cloud supercycle, as observed in the "31% sequential revenue growth for data center products in Q3, and we expect significant incremental volumes in the fourth quarter as well" albeit with a slight and temporary gross margin impact arising from "the capacity expansion for data center products."

2. Strategic Divestiture

As a result of Climate Solutions' richer gross profit margins of 24.8% in FQ3'26 (-3.8 points YoY/+5.5 from FY2019 levels of 19.3%, based on Commercial and Industrial Solutions and Building HVAC Systems), I can understand why MOD has decided to "spin off and combination of our Performance Technologies business with Gentherm."

This may allow the company to divest the slower-growth Performance Technologies (where vehicular end markets are embedded) at YTD revenue growth by -3.5% YoY compared to the Climate Solutions YTD growth by +28.7% YoY.

This is especially since Performance Technologies' YTD gross margins underwhelm at 18.6% (-1 point YoY/+4.8 from FY2019 levels of 13.8%) compared to Climate Solutions at 25.8% (-2.8 points YoY/+6.5 from FY2019 levels of 19.3%).

Pending the upcoming completion of the spinoff in Q4'26, I urge investors to temper their expectations from the near-term noise on MOD's performance metrics and the upcoming FY2027 guidance (to be offered in the upcoming FQ4'26 earnings call on May 20, 2026, post-market) from the slower growth Performance Technologies segment.

This is especially since MOD is likely to report an excellent pro forma performance metric upon the completed spin-off, as it "will become a pure-play climate solutions company, focused on the high-growth data center and commercial HVAC and refrigeration markets."

At the same time, readers may want to note that the spin-off will allow the management to use the $210M cash distribution to further reduce their debt leverage to a pro forma ratio of below 1x.

This is down from the current levels of 1.08x (compared to a year ago at 0.82x) based on the net debt levels of $517.1M in FQ3'26 (-80.1% YoY) and the annualized FQ3'26 adj. EBITDA of $478.4M (+36.9% YoY).

3. Profitable Growth Story Meets Expensive Valuations

Based on the:

  • YTD overall revenue performance of $2.22B (+15% YoY) and YTD adj. EPS performance of $3.31 (+12.9% YoY), aided by
  • the management's multi-year guidance of data center revenue growth at between +50% and +70%, and
  • the data center sales guidance of $2B in FY2028 (up from $1B guided for FY2026),

It is unsurprising that the consensus has already raised their forward estimates, with MOD expected to report accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +20.9%/+35.9% through FY2028.

This is compared to the historical growth profile at a 5Y CAGR of +5.5%/+31% and a 10Y CAGR of +5.6%/+20.85%, respectively, with it apparent that the data center capex boom has been a boon for its growth prospects.

For now, despite the -17% correction from the 52-week highs and the raised consensus forward estimates, I am of the opinion that MOD still looks rather stretched at FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 25.44x, given the ongoing upgrading from the 5Y mean of 11.38x and the sector median of 11.42x.

On the one hand, there is no denying its AI beneficiary status, as observed in the Climate Solutions' richer adj. EBITDA margins of 18.1% on a YTD basis (-2.8 points YoY), compared to the 11.4% observed in FY2021 and 11.7% in FY2020.

This is on top of the projected expansion to the 20% and 21% range in FQ4'26, with it signaling the company's highly profitable operations after the completion of the spin-off.

On the other hand, when comparing MOD's FWD EV/EBITDA valuations of 25.44x and Climate Solutions' projected FQ4'26 adj. EBITDA guidance of 20.5% at the midpoint to its direct thermal management competitors, including:

  • AAON, Inc. (AAON) at 21.20x/16% in FY2025,
  • Johnson Controls (JCI) at 19.72x/17.1% over the LTM,
  • Trane Technologies (TT) at 19.85x/20.1% in FY2025,
  • Vertiv Holdings Co. (VRT) at 29.81x/20.3% in FY2025,
  • Carrier Global (CARR) at 12.18x/20.9% in FY2025, and
  • Lennox International Inc. (LII) at 13.73x/22.1% in FY2025, respectively,

It is apparent that MOD's recent rally by +212% from the 52-week lows and by +39.4% from my last Buy article has occurred overly fast and furiously, with the premium valuations offering interested investors a minimal margin of safety.

MOD Is Downgraded To A Hold Here

For now, MOD's double top bull trap has materialized by February 2026, with the two up/downtrend cycles observed in 2024/Q1'25 and Q2'25-December '25/January 2026 implying another up/downtrend cycle playing out at the time of writing.

This conjecture is not overly bearish as well, based on the notably reversing momentum after the prior overbought levels in mid-February 2026, with a further correction along the established base uptrend support line/200-day moving averages rather likely in the near term.

This is especially since the stock is still trading at a notable premium to my fair value estimates of $138.90, based on the annualized FQ3'26 adj. EPS of $4.76 (+29.3% YoY) and my last Buy rating FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 29.18x (not too far from the peer group current P/E mean of ~31x and the peer group 5Y P/E mean of ~26x).

Based on the consensus FY2028 adj. EPS estimates of $10.16, it is apparent that the stock has also pulled forward part of its upside potential to my long-term price target of $296.40, with a further correction necessary for an improved margin of safety.

Readers may want to note that MOD still boasts a relatively elevated short interest ratio of 7.15% as the stock market sentiments remain extremely pessimistic by the time of writing - with it implying potential volatility in the near term, pending further catalysts and/or the upcoming Q2'26 earnings season in April/May 2026.

Combined with the potential correction by -13.1% to my Buy Zones of between the March 2026 bottom of $186s and/or the Q4'25 top of $165s (still elevated compared to my fair value estimates of $138s), I prefer to cautiously downgrade the stock to a Hold here.

Some patience may be more prudent in the meantime.

data.title: Quanta Services: Leading The Way For U.S. Reindustrialization (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Quanta Services, Inc. (PWR) operates in one of the most robust market environments for engineering, procurement, and construction (EPC) work, underpinned by billions of dollars in large-scale data center development, U.S. reindustrialization, and the power infrastructure to support grid scalability. With the utilities expected to invest $1.1T through 2030 in power infrastructure, I believe Quanta is well positioned to realize substantial growth over the coming years, if not the next decade, that can deliver incremental improvements to margins. Given the strong market outlook, I am recommending PWR shares with a Strong Buy rating with a price target of $713/share at 26.76x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

Quanta Services Operational Update

The EPC market is the strongest it has ever been, with a vast amount of large-scale projects set to deliver growth over the next decade. While data center development has been front and center in recent years as a large growth market opportunity, industrial reshoring for pharmaceuticals, manufacturing, automotive production, and semiconductor fabrication are all driving elevated demand for construction and infrastructure services. The central theme is that the growth trajectory for reindustrialization is outpacing load capacity, resulting in the need for substantial investments for power transmission & distribution infrastructure development.

Thematically, I have reason to believe natural gas will be the cornerstone of growth for the coming years, given the growing demand for gas-fired power generation. Accordingly, GE Vernova (GEV) had 83GW gas turbine capacity in its backlog and slot reservations as of Q4 ’25, up from 62GW in Q3 ’25 and 55GW in Q2 ’25. While capacity largely ties to large-scale data center development, much of this is destined for grid support and industrial demand.

With an increasing outlook for gas power demand, Quanta should be in a strong position to service both the high-voltage electrical equipment for the utility and also the distribution and substation equipment at the data center site, as well as the gas pipeline to ensure stable load capacity. At these facilities, Quanta will focus most heavily on the mechanical, electrical, and plumbing (MEP) components of the projects. To deliver on this growth, Quanta has planned $500-700mm in supply chain investments to double its existing high-voltage transformer manufacturing capacity by 2028.

Bolstering its position in mechanical and process infrastructure, Quanta acquired Dynamic Systems in mid-2025, solidifying its exposure in the technology, semiconductor, healthcare, and other growth markets in the U.S. Accordingly, the acquisition enhanced Quanta’s supply chain for preconstruction fabrication and aftermarket services.

Quanta recently announced a major development, deepening its partnership with NiSource (NI) as part of Quanta’s total solutions power generation platform, providing an integrated approach to power generation and supporting infrastructure. In October 2025, Quanta was selected to demonstrate this platform with NiSource for the design, procurement, and construction of 3GW of load capacity, expanding its generation and grid infrastructure in power generation, battery energy storage, transmission, substations, and underground infrastructure to support a large-scale data center campus in Indiana. This project includes the delivery of combined-cycle gas turbines. The project is expected to commence in 2026 following regulatory approvals and is expected to be completed by 2032. The project will be undertaken as a 50/50 joint venture between Quanta and Zachry Group, proportionally splitting financial contributions through the JV. According to commentary provided at Quanta’s 2026 Investor Day, the partnership can deliver $5-7b over the course of 5-7 years.

Looking at the total market opportunity, management estimates the total addressable market from 2026-2030 will be $2.365T, up from $970b in the previous 4 years. A substantial proportion of the TAM resides in electrical distribution and transmission, two core markets Quanta operates in. This is, in part, underpinned by aging infrastructure that may need to be modernized to support higher power capacity required to facilitate electrification and load diversification.

At the macroeconomic level, high-voltage equipment and construction labor are presently in short supply, potentially pushing out projects over longer durations if equipment and personnel cannot be adequately sourced. This, in part, is a large value driver for Quanta’s investment in its high-voltage transformer manufacturing capacity, which may be well timed to meet new capacity needs in the market. Given the shortage in electrical equipment, Quanta could potentially see positive pricing power for infrastructure that can potentially drive margins over the coming quarters.

Quanta Services Financial Position & Forecast

Looking ahead to Q1 ’26, I’m forecasting Quanta to generate $7.3b in net revenue with an adjusted EPS of $2.28/share. With the strong market opportunity at hand, I’m forecasting Quanta’s growth to remain durable for eFY26, particularly when considering its $44b backlog.

Looking at the balance sheet, Quanta remains well capitalized with $440mm in cash & equivalents and $6b in debt on the balance sheet for a net leverage ratio of 1.93x net debt/aEBITDA, within range of management’s 1.5-2x leverage target ratio. This should provide the firm with exceptional flexibility to continue its growth trajectory through organic reinvestments and targeted acquisitions to bolster its power transmission capabilities.

Risks Related to Quanta Services

Bull Case

Quanta has a rich backlog predicated on substantial demand for power infrastructure, supported by a multi-year data center build-out theme. Through both organic and inorganic growth, I believe Quanta will have the scale to take on high-growth, high-margin opportunities across the power value chain.

Bear Case

Uncertainty in the market remains heightened as to how much growth remains in the AI data center market. If growth in the market were to wane, I believe power infrastructure will follow suit, given that a large proportion of the new capacity expected to come online over the coming years is tied to large-scale data center development. A tight labor market may hinder growth in the near future.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

PWR shares are currently trading at 30.90x EV/aEBITDA, a relatively rich premium when compared to the shares’ historical trading range. Despite the elevated premium, I believe the market sees heightened growth for the EPC firm, underpinned by an expected $1.1T in power infrastructure investments by 2030 to support reindustrialization and data center development.

With a deep backlog and high demand for critical infrastructure, I believe Quanta is well positioned to further broaden its scope in these high-growth markets. I am recommending PWR shares with a Strong Buy rating with a price target of $713/share at 26.76x eFY28 EV/aEBITDA.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.
